Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

U. T. Place & J. J. C. Smart

mid-20th c.

Consciousness is a brain process: the rise of mind-brain identity theory.

The philosophy of mind that can be extracted from U.T. Place and J.J.C. Smart is not the crude slogan “the mind is the brain.” It is a more exact position: many ordinary mental concepts retain their ordinary psychological and behavioral roles, but conscious episodes, sensations, images, and experiences are to be identified with brain processes, not merely correlated with them. The result is an anti-Cartesian, anti-sense-datum, empirically constrained physicalism: mental discourse is not eliminated, translated into neuroscience, or treated as meaningless, but the events that make conscious experience possible are held to be physical events in the nervous system. Place and Smart therefore mark a decisive transition from behaviorist and ordinary-language treatments of mind to modern physicalist philosophy of mind.

The reconstruction below distinguishes three things: Place’s own position, Smart’s own position, and the more comprehensive philosophy of mind that emerges when their views are combined.

1. Historical position: why Place and Smart matter

Before Place and Smart, mid-twentieth-century Anglophone philosophy of mind was dominated by two pressures. The first was anti-Cartesian ordinary-language philosophy, especially Gilbert Ryle’s attack on the “ghost in the machine” picture. Ryle treated many mental concepts not as names of hidden inner episodes but as ways of characterizing intelligent, dispositional, and norm-governed behavior. The second was behaviorism in psychology and philosophy, according to which psychology should focus on behavior, and in stronger philosophical forms, mental terms could be analyzed or translated into behavioral terms.

Place and Smart accepted much of the anti-Cartesian impulse but rejected the idea that all mental discourse could be handled behavioristically. Place’s decisive move was to say that Rylean or dispositional analyses work fairly well for concepts such as believing, knowing, intending, wanting, understanding, and remembering, but not for consciousness, sensation, mental imagery, or occurrent experience. These latter items form what Place called an “intractable residue” for behaviorism, and he proposed that the residue should be treated as inner processes, without thereby returning to dualism.

This is why Place’s 1956 paper, “Is Consciousness a Brain Process?”, and Smart’s 1959 paper, “Sensations and Brain Processes,” became canonical. Together with Herbert Feigl’s work, they provided the early architecture of the modern mind-brain identity theory. Place later described the three texts by Place, Feigl, and Smart as the “three primary sources” of the materialist or identity view, and later commentators describe Place’s 1956 paper as a watershed that set much of the agenda for philosophy of mind in the second half of the twentieth century.

Their historical role is therefore transitional. They are post-behaviorist because they reject an exclusively behavioral analysis of consciousness; they are pre-functionalist because they do not yet give the full causal-role theory later associated with Armstrong, Lewis, Putnam, and functionalism; and they are foundational for physicalism because they make it plausible that mental events can be identical with physical events without mental language meaning the same thing as neurophysiological language.

2. Place’s philosophy of mind

Place’s central contribution is best understood as a divided account of the mental. He does not identify every mental state with a brain state. Instead, he distinguishes dispositional mental states from occurrent mental events. Beliefs, desires, intentions, and many cognitive-volitional concepts can often be understood as dispositions or capacities: patterns of likely behavior, response, inference, verbal report, and practical adjustment. Conscious episodes, sensations, mental images, and acts of attending are different. They are not adequately captured by behavioral dispositions alone; they involve inner processes, and Place’s proposal is that those inner processes are brain processes.

This divided view was already visible in Place’s 1954 essay “The Concept of Heed.” There he examined attention and consciousness against the background of Rylean behaviorism. He accepted that many mental concepts are assessed through behavior and cross-examination, and he accepted dispositional accounts for many intellectual and volitional concepts. But he resisted applying the same analysis to attending, observing, and being conscious. His reason was that some phenomena require reference to an internal state or process that makes the relevant behavioral disposition possible.

That point matters because it shows that Place’s later identity theory was not a sudden abandonment of behaviorism. It was a correction of behaviorism from within. Place wanted to preserve what behaviorism had taught: mental terms do not simply name private Cartesian objects, and psychological explanation must be anchored in behavior, report, and observable capacities. But he also thought behaviorism became dogmatic if it denied the existence of genuine inner processes involved in consciousness.

The most famous thesis of Place’s 1956 paper is that the statement “consciousness is a brain process” should be treated as a reasonable scientific hypothesis, not as a conceptual truth and not as an obvious contradiction. He explicitly rejects the idea that sensation statements are translatable into brain-process statements. The claim is not that “being conscious” means “having such-and-such neural process.” Rather, the claim is that the process picked out in ordinary language as consciousness may be the very same process described by neuroscience.

Place’s key distinction is between the “is” of definition and the “is” of composition or constitution. “A square is an equilateral rectangle” is definitional; “lightning is a motion of electric charges” is not definitional in that way. A person can know what lightning is in ordinary experience without knowing anything about electrical discharge. Yet scientific inquiry can discover that the phenomenon encountered as lightning is identical with a physical process. Place applies this model to consciousness: ordinary experiential language and neurophysiological language can have different meanings, different criteria of use, and different methods of verification, while still referring to one and the same event or process.

This gives Place a powerful answer to one of the standard objections to materialism: that consciousness and brain processes are logically independent, and therefore cannot be identical. Place’s reply is that logical independence of descriptions does not entail ontological independence of things described. “Cloud” and “mass of droplets” do not mean the same thing, but the cloud may be nothing over and above the droplets. Similarly, “conscious experience” and “brain process” need not mean the same thing for conscious experience to be a brain process.

Place also rejects the idea that mere correlation is enough. The identity theory is not the weak thesis that conscious experiences are regularly accompanied by neural events. It is the stronger thesis that the neural event explains the observations and reports associated with the conscious event in the way the motion of electric charges explains the observable features of lightning. The scientific task is therefore to show that introspective reports and behavioral evidence can be explained by brain processes of the relevant sort.

The most original semantic and phenomenological part of Place’s view is his diagnosis of the “phenomenological fallacy.” According to this diagnosis, philosophers are misled by the grammar of experience reports. When someone says they have a green after-image, it is tempting to suppose that there exists an inner green object, a private image, or a phenomenal item literally possessing greenness. Place argues that this is a mistake. The report does not describe a green object in an inner theater; rather, it reports having a sort of experience normally associated with seeing green things.

This move lets Place reject sense-datum theory and Cartesian inner-object ontology. Experiences do not populate a private mental space with colored, shaped, ghostly items. Instead, ordinary phenomenal language is parasitic on public object-language. We learn to describe colors, shapes, sounds, and movements in the world; then we use those terms, with qualifications such as “looks,” “seems,” “appears,” or “feels,” to report how things are presented to us in experience. The green after-image is not an inner green thing; it is a brain process that causes the subject to make a report using the vocabulary normally associated with green patches.

Place’s later reflections confirm that his identity theory remained restricted. He did not become a full-scope central-state materialist of the kind later associated with Armstrong. In 1988 he emphasized that his 1956 thesis concerned mental events, sensations, images, thought occurrences, and associated activities, not mental states in general. Mental states such as beliefs and desires are dispositions, not brain states, although their existence may depend causally on internal structures.

This restriction is philosophically important because it addresses intentionality. Beliefs and desires have content: they are about objects, facts, situations, or possibilities. Place was skeptical of identifying such content-bearing states straightforwardly with neural microstructure. His solution was to locate intentionality primarily in dispositions, abilities, behavioral tendencies, and environmental relations rather than in occurrent inner objects. Thus Place’s philosophy is not a simple “everything mental is neural” thesis. It is a hybrid: occurrent conscious processes are brain processes, while many standing mental states are dispositional structures embedded in organism-environment interaction.

Place’s metaphilosophical ambition was also distinctive. He wanted to remove bad philosophical objections so that the mind-body problem could be handed over to empirical psychology, neurophysiology, and later neuroscience. The philosopher’s role is not to discover by armchair reflection which neural process is consciousness, but to show that there is no conceptual barrier to such an identification and to specify what would count as evidence for it.

3. Smart’s philosophy of mind

Smart’s contribution is more explicitly metaphysical and semantic. He begins from Place’s suggestion that sensations may be brain processes and develops a sharper defense of strict identity. His thesis is not that “pain,” “after-image,” or “sensation” means “brain process.” It is that the event reported in sensation language may be the very same event that neuroscience describes as a brain process. Smart insists that the identity is strict: sensations are not merely caused by, correlated with, accompanied by, or represented by brain processes; they are brain processes.

Smart’s argument relies heavily on the distinction between meaning and reference. A person can know that they are having an after-image without knowing anything about neurophysiology, just as someone can see lightning without knowing that lightning is an electrical discharge. This does not show that the after-image experience is nonphysical. It shows only that the ordinary expression and the scientific expression are not synonymous.

Smart develops this into an answer to the “contingency” objection. Critics say: if sensations were brain processes, then “this sensation is a brain process” would have to be necessary or analytic; but it is not analytic, so the identity is false. Smart’s answer is that genuine identity statements can be contingent discoveries expressed by non-synonymous terms. “Lightning is an electrical discharge” is not analytic; it is a scientific identification. Likewise, “this sensation is such-and-such brain process” need not be analytic in order to be true.

Smart’s most famous technical device is the idea of “topic-neutral” language. When someone reports an after-image, the report need not describe a private nonphysical object. It can be analyzed as saying, roughly, “there is something going on in me that is like what goes on when I see a yellowish-orange patch.” The phrase “something going on” does not specify whether the event is mental, physical, neural, or non-neural. It is neutral with respect to the ultimate topic or ontology. This lets Smart explain how ordinary subjects can know and report their experiences without knowing the scientific nature of those experiences.

Smart’s topic-neutral strategy is closely related to Place’s phenomenological fallacy diagnosis, but it is more explicitly semantic. Place says philosophers mistakenly reify the apparent objects of experience; Smart says the correct analysis of experience reports need not commit us to inner objects or nonphysical qualities. The report is about an occurrence, but the occurrence may be specified only by its causal, comparative, or phenomenal role. It is then open to science to identify the occurrence with a neural process.

Smart also addresses the objection that brain processes do not have the properties sensations appear to have. A brain process is not yellow, orange, sharp, sweet, or painful in the ordinary object-property sense. Smart’s answer is that one must not confuse the properties of what is represented or normally perceived with the properties of the experience itself. The experience of a yellowish-orange after-image is not itself a yellowish-orange object; it is an event describable by reference to the sort of perceptual situation in which yellowish-orange things are normally seen.

Smart differs from Place in how he treats the status of materialism. Place presented consciousness-brain identity as a scientific hypothesis to be confirmed by neurophysiology and psychology. Smart agreed that the specific identification of a sensation with a particular brain process is empirical, but he was more skeptical that any experiment could decide between materialism and epiphenomenalism if both theories predict the same correlations. For Smart, the deeper reason to prefer materialism is parsimony: dualism introduces extra psychophysical laws and nonphysical entities, which he called, following Feigl’s phrase, “nomological danglers.”

This parsimony argument is central to Smart. If the physical story explains behavior, report, and the neural mechanisms of experience, then adding nonphysical experiences as separate causal or nomological accompaniments is metaphysically extravagant. It posits additional facts that do no explanatory work. Smart’s materialism is therefore an ontological economy argument: accept the ontology required by science and reject surplus nonphysical accompaniments.

Smart’s position is sometimes called “central-state materialism” because it identifies sensations with central nervous system states or processes. But in his early paper the focus is especially on sensations and experiences rather than the whole range of intentional mental states. The later expansion to all mental states, including beliefs and desires, is more characteristic of Armstrong and later central-state materialists, though Smart’s work helped make that expansion possible.

4. Shared core: the Place-Smart identity theory

The shared Place-Smart view can be summarized as follows. First, they reject Cartesian substance dualism. There is no need to posit a nonphysical mental substance standing alongside the body. Second, they reject reductive behaviorism as a complete account of consciousness. Some mental concepts are dispositional, but conscious experiences are genuine internal events. Third, they reject sense-datum theories and private-object ontologies. Experience reports do not force us to posit nonphysical phenomenal objects. Fourth, they defend mind-brain identity for conscious episodes: the event reported introspectively is identical with a neural process described scientifically.

The shared view is also anti-translationist. They do not claim that ordinary mental language can be translated into neurophysiological language. This is a crucial point. Place and Smart are not saying that “pain” means “C-fiber firing,” or that “having an after-image” means “having neural process N.” They are saying that the referent or realizer of the experience may be a brain process, even though the language by which the subject reports it is not neuroscientific.

The shared view is therefore ontologically reductive but not semantically reductive. It reduces what there is, not how we speak. Mental vocabulary remains useful because it tracks phenomena from the first-person, behavioral, and psychological standpoint. Neuroscientific vocabulary tracks the same events from the standpoint of physical mechanism. The vocabularies differ, but the world need not contain two kinds of event.

This is one of their most durable contributions. Modern physicalism still often distinguishes ontological reduction from conceptual reduction. One may hold that every mental event is physical without holding that mental concepts are synonymous with physical concepts, or that psychology should be replaced by neuroscience. Place and Smart helped make that distinction central to philosophy of mind.

5. Place versus Smart: the important differences

Place and Smart are often grouped together, but their positions are not identical.

Place is more cautious and more closely tied to psychology. He treats the identity claim as a scientific hypothesis: once philosophers remove confusions about language and introspection, empirical research must discover which brain processes correspond to and explain conscious experience. He also preserves a strong dispositional account of many mental states. For Place, beliefs, desires, intentions, and other standing states are not simply brain states; they are dispositions grounded in the organism’s structure and history.

Smart is more explicitly metaphysical. He stresses strict identity, meaning/reference distinctions, topic-neutral analysis, and parsimony. He is less confident than Place that empirical evidence alone can decide between materialism and certain dualist rivals, because an epiphenomenalist could accept all the same correlations while adding nonphysical accompaniments. Smart therefore argues that materialism is preferable because it avoids unnecessary nomological additions.

The difference can be put this way: Place’s guiding model is scientific integration; Smart’s guiding model is ontological economy. Place asks, “Can neurophysiology explain the observations associated with consciousness?” Smart asks, “Why posit anything beyond the physical processes needed to explain behavior and report?” These are complementary, but not identical, arguments.

They also differ over the scope of identity. Place explicitly resists a full identity theory for all mental states. Smart’s early argument focuses on sensations, but his style of materialism pushes more naturally toward a broader central-state view. The later development of Australian materialism, especially in Armstrong, extends the identity strategy to mental states generally; Place remained more resistant to that extension.

6. Extracting a comprehensive philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Place-Smart philosophy of mind can be formulated in twelve theses.

First, the ontology is physicalist. The world contains organisms, nervous systems, behavior, environments, and physical processes. It does not require a separate mental substance, a private phenomenal realm, or irreducible nonphysical qualia. Consciousness is not outside nature; it is a natural process within the organism.

Second, the mental domain is heterogeneous. Not every mental concept functions in the same way. Some mental concepts pick out dispositions, abilities, tendencies, and capacities. Others report occurrent episodes. The mistake of both Cartesianism and crude behaviorism is to treat “the mental” as one uniform category. Place’s contribution is to divide the field: belief, intention, and desire are not philosophically identical in structure to pain, after-image, or sensory consciousness.

Third, conscious episodes are real internal events. Against eliminative or reductive behaviorism, Place and Smart do not deny that pains, images, sensations, and experiences occur. The point is not that consciousness is a fiction. The point is that the events which ordinary subjects report as conscious experiences are physical events in the brain.

Fourth, identity is not translation. Mental terms and neural terms differ in meaning, use, epistemic access, and criteria of application. Nevertheless, they may refer to the same event. This is the core semantic lesson of the identity theory. The distinction between meaning and reference allows the identity theorist to say that a subject can know they are in pain without knowing the neurophysiology of pain.

Fifth, introspection is evidence, not revelation. First-person reports are important data for psychology, but they do not reveal the metaphysical nature of experience. A subject may be authoritative about how things seem while mistaken about what kind of process the seeming is. This is analogous to being able to identify lightning perceptually without knowing its electrical constitution.

Sixth, phenomenal language is object-involving and derivative. When we describe an after-image as green or a pain as sharp, we should not infer that there is a private green object or a private sharp object inside the mind. We are using language learned in public perceptual contexts to classify experiences by their normal causes, similarities, and effects. This is Place’s phenomenological fallacy argument and Smart’s topic-neutral strategy in a shared form.

Seventh, psychology is not eliminated by neuroscience. Ordinary psychological vocabulary remains indispensable for describing behavior, dispositions, perception, action, learning, and report. But psychology must be compatible with neurophysiology, and where psychology identifies occurrent conscious processes, neuroscience may identify their physical nature. This makes Place and Smart reductive about ontology but pluralistic about explanatory levels.

Eighth, mental causation is preserved by identity. If pains, sensations, and conscious episodes are brain processes, then they can cause behavior in the ordinary physical way that brain processes cause behavior. This avoids the epiphenomenalist problem: if conscious events were nonphysical accompaniments with no causal role, then their relation to reports and actions would become mysterious. Place later pressed this point against epiphenomenalism and psychophysical parallelism.

Ninth, intentionality should not be assimilated too quickly to sensation. Place’s caution is valuable here. Beliefs and desires are content-bearing and often world-involving; their individuation depends on behavior, linguistic practice, environmental relations, and norms of rationality. A Place-style philosophy therefore treats intentional states more dispositionally or functionally than it treats raw sensory episodes.

Tenth, the correct explanatory program is integrative. The mind should be studied through the coordination of first-person reports, behavioral evidence, psychological theory, and neurophysiology. Reports are neither infallible metaphysical windows nor meaningless noises. They are behaviorally and linguistically structured data whose production should itself be explained by brain processes.

Eleventh, materialism is justified both empirically and theoretically. Place emphasizes empirical confirmation: neuroscience should identify the brain processes that explain conscious reports and behavior. Smart emphasizes theoretical economy: once the physical story does the explanatory work, dualist additions are unnecessary “nomological danglers.” A robust Place-Smart physicalism combines both lines: explanatory integration plus ontological parsimony.

Twelfth, philosophy’s task is therapeutic and methodological. It should dissolve misleading pictures—Cartesian inner theaters, private sense-data, synonymy requirements, and the idea that first-person certainty entails nonphysical ontology. Once these confusions are removed, the substantive investigation of consciousness belongs increasingly to empirical psychology and neuroscience.

7. The resulting theory of consciousness

The Place-Smart account of consciousness can be stated in a rigorous form:

A conscious experience is an occurrent internal process in an organism, ordinarily reportable by the subject in public phenomenal vocabulary, functionally connected with perception, attention, memory, discrimination, and behavior, and identical with a brain process discoverable by empirical science.

This formulation is intentionally layered. “Occurrent” distinguishes experiences from standing dispositions. “Internal process” distinguishes consciousness from mere behavioral tendency. “Ordinarily reportable” connects consciousness to the first-person and psychological evidence base. “Public phenomenal vocabulary” blocks private-language and sense-datum reification. “Functionally connected” recognizes the role of experience in perception and behavior. “Identical with a brain process” states the ontological thesis.

On this view, a pain is not a ghostly object, a nonphysical property, or a behavioral disposition alone. It is a neural process that is ordinarily caused by bodily damage or disturbance, tends to produce aversive behavior and verbal report, occupies a certain role in the organism’s control system, and is reported by the subject through pain-language. The word “pain” does not mean “neural process N,” but the pain may be neural process N.

Likewise, an after-image is not a colored item in a private mental space. It is an experience describable by reference to public perceptual situations. To report a yellowish-orange after-image is to report that something is going on in one that is like what goes on when one sees a yellowish-orange patch, not to assert the existence of a private yellowish-orange object.

This position is austere, but it is not eliminativist. It does not deny that pain hurts or that after-images appear colored. Rather, it denies that the hurting or appearing requires nonphysical objects or properties. The subjective character is real as a feature of the event, but the event is physical.

8. The resulting theory of psychology

Place and Smart also imply a distinctive philosophy of psychology. Psychology should not be reduced to behaviorism, because behavior alone does not capture the internal processes involved in perception, attention, sensation, imagery, and consciousness. But psychology should not become introspective dualism either, because introspective reports do not disclose private nonphysical entities. The correct psychology is a science connecting stimuli, behavior, report, learning history, attention, discrimination, and neural process.

Place’s professional background makes this especially salient. He was trained in philosophy, psychology, and physiology, worked as a psychologist, and remained engaged with behaviorism and behavior analysis. His identity theory is therefore not just metaphysics; it is a proposal about how psychology can remain scientifically respectable while taking consciousness seriously.

The Place-Smart psychology has three explanatory levels. At the behavioral level, we observe action, discrimination, verbal report, avoidance, preference, and practical adjustment. At the psychological level, we classify capacities, dispositions, experiences, and perceptual conditions. At the neural level, we identify the physical processes that realize conscious episodes and support dispositions. The levels are not rival ontologies. They are different descriptions of one organismic system.

This also explains why first-person reports have a special status without requiring Cartesian certainty. Reports such as “I am in pain” or “I see a green after-image” are normally treated as authoritative because of the rules and practices governing psychological language, not because the subject inspects a private nonphysical object. In difficult cases, neuroscience and behavioral evidence may eventually revise or explain the conditions under which such reports are made.

9. The later development of the field

Place and Smart changed the trajectory of philosophy of mind. Before them, the dominant anti-dualist alternatives were often behaviorist or ordinary-language approaches. After them, materialism became a central live option. Their work opened the way for Armstrong’s central-state materialism, Lewis-style functionalism, later token physicalism, nonreductive physicalism, and contemporary debates about consciousness and qualia.

The first major pressure came from multiple realizability. Hilary Putnam argued that the same mental state type could be realized by different physical states in different species, organisms, or systems. If pain in humans, octopuses, Martians, and artificial systems is realized by different physical mechanisms, then a simple global type identity between pain and one neural state becomes implausible. This objection helped push the field toward functionalism and token identity theories.

There are several Place-Smart responses. One is to restrict identity claims by species, structure, or context: human pain may be identical with one kind of human neural process, while pain in another species is identical with a different physical process. Another is to shift from type identity to token identity: each particular pain is physical, even if there is no single physical type common to all pains. Later discussions of identity theory explore these restricted or more flexible versions.

The second major pressure came from modal and conceivability objections, especially associated with Kripke and later anti-physicalist arguments about qualia. If “pain” and a neural process term are rigid designators, then if pain is identical with that neural process, the identity should be necessary. But many philosophers find it conceivable that pain could exist without that neural process, or that the neural process could exist without pain. This challenge attacks the analogy with ordinary scientific identities and remains one of the major problems for identity theory.

The third pressure came from the explanatory gap. Even if neuroscience identifies the mechanisms associated with pain, vision, attention, and report, critics argue that the subjective character of experience—what it is like—has not been fully explained. Place and Smart would likely respond that much of this objection rests on the phenomenological fallacy or on reifying qualities into private objects. But the persistence of the explanatory-gap problem shows that their treatment of phenomenal character, while foundational, did not end the debate.

The fourth pressure concerns intentionality. Place’s own restriction of identity theory to mental events looks prescient here. Beliefs, desires, memories, and intentions are individuated partly by content, rational relations, social practice, and environment. Later externalist and functionalist theories developed these themes in ways that go beyond early identity theory. Place’s divided account can accommodate some of this by treating intentional states as dispositions or organism-environment relations rather than as simple inner episodes.

10. Philosophical evaluation

The enduring strength of Place and Smart is that they broke a false dilemma. Before them, one might think the choices were Cartesian dualism, which preserves inner experience at the cost of a mysterious nonphysical realm, or behaviorism, which preserves scientific respectability at the cost of seeming to flatten consciousness into behavior. Place and Smart show a third option: accept inner conscious events, but identify them with brain processes.

Their second achievement is semantic. They make it clear that an identity claim need not be a synonymy claim. This distinction is now basic to philosophy of mind. Without it, materialism looks obviously false: “pain” does not mean “neural firing.” With it, materialism becomes philosophically serious: pain may be neural firing even though ordinary pain-language and neuroscience have different meanings, methods, and uses.

Their third achievement is methodological. They reposition the mind-body problem as partly empirical. Philosophers should not decide from the armchair that consciousness cannot be physical. They should clarify the concepts, expose fallacies, and then let psychology and neuroscience investigate the mechanisms. This is especially Place’s contribution.

Their main weakness is that the original identity theory underestimates the complexity of mental kinds. “Pain,” “belief,” “visual experience,” and “attention” may not map neatly onto single neural types. Later functionalism, multiple-realizability arguments, and cognitive science made this much harder to sustain in a simple type-identity form.

Their second weakness is that the treatment of phenomenal character remains controversial. Place’s phenomenological fallacy and Smart’s topic-neutral analysis are powerful against sense-datum theories, but many philosophers think they do not fully explain why physical processes feel like anything at all. This is the ancestor of later qualia and explanatory-gap debates.

Their third weakness is that early identity theory was neurophysiologically unspecific. It said sensations are brain processes but did not provide detailed accounts of which processes, at what level of neural organization, under which computational or functional descriptions. This is not a decisive objection, because Place explicitly treated the details as empirical work for psychology and neurophysiology. But it means the theory is more a metaphysical and methodological framework than a completed science of consciousness.

11. Final synthesis

The comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Place and Smart is best called empirical mind-brain identity physicalism with a divided theory of mental concepts.

It says that conscious episodes are real but physical; that introspection is genuine evidence but not metaphysical revelation; that mental and neural language need not be synonymous to be co-referential; that many cognitive and intentional states are better understood dispositionally or functionally; that phenomenal-object talk is often a misleading projection from public perceptual language; that psychology should integrate behavior, report, and neurophysiology; and that dualist additions are unnecessary unless they do explanatory work that physical theory cannot do.

Place gives the view its psychological discipline: a careful division between dispositions and occurrent conscious processes, a scientific hypothesis about consciousness, and a critique of the phenomenological fallacy. Smart gives the view its metaphysical sharpness: strict identity, meaning/reference distinctions, topic-neutral reports, and the parsimony argument against dualism. Together they transform philosophy of mind from a debate between Cartesian introspection and behaviorist deflation into the modern debate over physicalism, consciousness, functional realization, qualia, and the explanatory scope of neuroscience.
